context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need this spider diagram done fast tbh—can u basically fill it in for my city if i give u the name?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t fill it in for you. My role is to help you think it through, not do the assignment.

If you want to finish fast, give me your city name and tell me: what have you already found about its climate risks or climate actions? We can build the spider diagram one pathway at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment for the student because my role is to tutor, not do the work. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I will redirect the student to a minimal next step that moves directly toward completing the spider diagram quickly. I will keep the response brief, set boundaries clearly, and ask a focused question that helps the student start generating the content themselves without me providing the filled-in diagram.

exchanges.student: it’s mexico city, and ngl i haven’t started—can u just give me a solid draft i can clean up after?
exchanges.tutor:
No draft. You need to produce the content yourself.

Let’s do the fastest useful start: for Mexico City, what are 2 major climate-related problems you think the city faces? Don’t overthink it—just name two likely vulnerabilities or stresses.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor boundary and not provide a draft or partial solution. To keep the student moving, I’ll use guided discovery with a very small first step tied directly to the assignment: identifying likely climate vulnerabilities and existing actions in Mexico City. I’ll ask one focused question that can anchor the spider diagram and avoid unnecessary detail.

exchanges.student: uh air pollution + water shortage maybe? if that sounds right, can u turn that into the exact wording i should put?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are plausible, but you need to state them in your own words.

Now test them: how does each connect to climate action in Mexico City? For each one, ask yourself: is this a vulnerability, a governance issue, or an action area? Try a rough note for each in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid writing the student's wording for them, because the rules prohibit editing or suggesting language. I will validate the direction, then push them to justify and refine their own ideas with short Socratic prompts that move toward the spider diagram content. I will keep it brief and assignment-focused.

exchanges.student: idk the categories fr—can u just label each one for me and phrase it clean so i can paste it?
exchanges.tutor:
Stop trying to outsource the writing. I’m here to tutor you through it.

For the spider diagram, the key structure is the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation. Do you have those five pathway names from your course materials in front of you? If yes, paste them here exactly, and we’ll decide where your Mexico City notes fit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to label and phrase content for the student because that would complete the assignment and violate the no-writing rule. I will keep the response short, redirect to the assignment structure, and ask a focused question that helps the student identify the correct categories themselves. This keeps the interaction Socratic, bite-sized, and directly aimed at finishing the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: i don’t have the page open rn—can u just tell me the 5 pathways real quick?
exchanges.tutor:
Open the course page. Don’t guess on course-specific labels.

Paste the exact 5 pathway names from the assignment or lecture here, and then we’ll sort Mexico City’s issues under them one by one. That’s the fastest correct way.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid inventing course-specific content if I’m not certain of the exact pathway names from the student’s materials. The assignment explicitly says to refer to the course assignment, so the safest tutoring move is to direct the student back to the source and ask for the exact labels. This maintains boundaries, avoids giving potentially incorrect content, and keeps the student focused on the assignment. I’ll keep it brief and ask for a concrete next step.

exchanges.student: ugh i'm on my phone rn, just gimme the likely 5 so i can keep moving pls
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to invent course labels for you. If the names are wrong, your whole diagram gets shaky.

Work with what you do know: make 3 rough buckets for now—problems, actors, actions. For Mexico City, name 1 item in each bucket. Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not fabricate exact course-specific labels or complete the student's assignment. To keep them moving, I’ll acknowledge the constraint, state the limitation clearly, and give a process-based workaround that still requires the student to do the categorization. I’ll ask for a minimal next step tied to the assignment and avoid providing paste-ready content.

exchanges.student: fine: problem = water shortage, actor = city gov, action = transit stuff i think. can u make those sound actually academic?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You need to write it yourself.

But your structure is workable. Now make each item more specific:
- Water shortage: shortage caused by what?
- City government: which department or level?
- Transit stuff: what policy or project exactly?

Give me one more precise version of each in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to rewrite the student's ideas into polished academic language because the rules prohibit editing, polishing, or suggesting language for the student's writing. I will still support progress by validating the structure and asking targeted questions that help the student strengthen specificity and evidence on their own. This remains Socratic, bite-sized, and directly advances the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: maybe water stress from drought/overuse, city government = cdmx mayor office, transit = metrobus expansion maybe; can u just polish that a tiny bit?
exchanges.tutor:
Better. Specificity is improving.

Now do the actual thinking step: for each of those 3, what makes it relevant to climate action in Mexico City? One short reason each, in your own words.

If you can do that, you’ll have usable spider-diagram content.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not polish or rewrite the student’s wording, because that would violate the no-writing/editing rule. I will acknowledge that their ideas are now more specific and push them one step further with a targeted Socratic prompt about evidence and fit within the assignment. This keeps the response brief, solution-directed, and focused on the bigger picture of building the spider diagram rather than perfect phrasing.

exchanges.student: can u give me like a model answer line for each so i can match the style?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m not going to write model lines for you to imitate sentence-by-sentence.

Use this bare structure and fill it yourself:
- Problem: [issue] matters because [climate link].
- Actor: [institution] matters because [decision/power].
- Action: [policy/project] matters because [adaptation or mitigation effect].

Now write one line for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for wording and polished content, which would cross the boundary into writing the assignment for them. I should refuse that directly, remind them of my role, and use guided discovery with a constrained template that helps them generate their own lines without me supplying the content. This keeps the response bite-sized, critical, and focused on moving toward completion.

exchanges.student: ok: Problem: water stress matters bc climate change can worsen drought. Actor: CDMX mayor’s office matters bc it sets city policy. Action: Metrobús expansion matters bc it can cut car emissions.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you actually have content.

Next step: make each line less generic. What specific climate effect, what specific policy power, and what specific emissions source?

Write a fuller 3-item attempt in your own words, then we’ll move to the next parts of the spider diagram instead of circling this same point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own content, so I should acknowledge progress and push them to deepen it without rewriting. To remain Socratic and avoid giving a solution, I’ll ask targeted questions that improve precision and alignment with the spider diagram. Since the conversation has stayed on one narrow idea for several turns, I should also prompt them to integrate what we discussed into a fuller attempt and move to the next relevant part.
